title: UCB — Epilepsy — Knowledge Doc
html:
Market Context: UCB Epilepsy Portfolio — Briviact, Fintepla, Nayzilam

Client: UCB Portfolio: Briviact (brivaracetam), Fintepla (fenfluramine), Nayzilam (midazolam nasal spray) Therapeutic area: Neurology — Epilepsy (focal seizures, rare epilepsy syndromes, acute seizure clusters) Document scope: Portfolio-level competitive and clinical landscape Last updated: April 2026

1. Epilepsy Landscape Overview

1.1 Epidemiology

Epilepsy affects approximately 3.4 million people in the United States, with about 150,000 new diagnoses annually. The condition is characterized by recurrent, unprovoked seizures caused by abnormal electrical activity in the brain. Key statistics:

  • Prevalence: ~1.2% of the US population; higher rates in children (<2 years) and older adults (>65 years)
  • Drug-resistant epilepsy: ~30-40% of patients fail to achieve seizure control with their first two appropriately chosen ASMs (antiseizure medications), meeting the ILAE definition of drug-resistant epilepsy
  • Rare developmental and epileptic encephalopathies (DEEs): Dravet syndrome (~1 in 15,700 births), Lennox-Gastaut syndrome (~1-2% of all childhood epilepsies), CDKL5 deficiency disorder (even rarer)
  • Seizure clusters: ~15-25% of epilepsy patients experience acute repetitive seizures / seizure clusters requiring rescue therapy

1.2 Market Segmentation — UCB's Three Plays

UCB's epilepsy portfolio operates across three distinct market segments, each with different competitive dynamics, HCP targets, and growth trajectories:

Segment Brand 2025 Revenue Growth Primary Competitor
Focal seizures (POS) Briviact (brivaracetam) ~EUR758M (+14% YoY) Mature growth XCOPRI (cenobamate), generic LEV
Rare DEEs (DS + LGS) Fintepla (fenfluramine) ~EUR427M (+30% YoY) High growth Epidiolex (cannabidiol)
Rescue / seizure clusters Nayzilam (midazolam nasal spray) Not disclosed separately Moderate VALTOCO (diazepam nasal spray)

Together, Briviact and Fintepla generated EUR1.2 billion ($1.3 billion) in net sales in 2025. UCB's total epilepsy portfolio is the commercial backbone alongside its immunology franchise (BIMZELX, CIMZIA).

1.3 Standard Treatment Algorithm — Focal Seizures

Diagnosis: Focal (partial-onset) seizures
  |
  v
1L: Monotherapy — generic levetiracetam (Keppra), lamotrigine, or carbamazepine
  |
  ├── Adequate seizure control? → Continue
  |
  └── Inadequate? (failed 1-2 ASMs)
        |
        v
      Adjunctive therapy options:
        ├── BRIVIACT (brivaracetam) — SV2A, branded, clean tolerability
        ├── XCOPRI (cenobamate) — dual MOA (Na+ channel + GABA), aggressive marketing
        ├── FYCOMPA (perampanel) — AMPA antagonist
        ├── APTIOM (eslicarbazepine) — Na+ channel
        ├── Lacosamide/Vimpat (generic 2023) — Na+ channel
        └── Other older generics

Key insight: The POS space is highly genericized. Briviact's positioning is not as a first-line agent but as a preferred branded add-on or switch from levetiracetam. The differentiation story is about the SV2A selectivity advantage over levetiracetam (better tolerability, especially behavioral/psychiatric AEs) while maintaining efficacy.

2. Briviact (Brivaracetam) — The SV2A Story

2.1 Product Profile

  • Generic name: Brivaracetam
  • Mechanism: High-affinity selective synaptic vesicle protein 2A (SV2A) ligand — 15-30x higher affinity for SV2A than levetiracetam
  • FDA approval: February 2016 (adults); expanded to patients >=1 month of age
  • Indication: Treatment of partial-onset (focal) seizures in patients 1 month of age and older
  • Formulations: Oral tablets (25mg, 50mg, 75mg, 100mg), oral solution, IV injection
  • Dosing: 50-200 mg/day (adults), weight-based for pediatric; notably does NOT require titration in adults — can start at therapeutic dose
  • 2025 global revenue: EUR758 million ($890M USD), up 14% YoY

2.2 The SV2A Differentiation vs Keppra (Levetiracetam)

This is the core commercial narrative. Both brivaracetam and levetiracetam are racetams that bind to SV2A, but the clinical implications are different:

Why Briviact is not "just another Keppra":

  1. Higher SV2A affinity (15-30x): Brivaracetam has a more selective, higher-affinity interaction with SV2A. It does NOT hit the AMPA receptor, which levetiracetam does — and AMPA activity is thought to drive levetiracetam's well-known behavioral/psychiatric side effects (aggression, irritability, "Keppra rage")
  2. Behavioral/cognitive stability: Clinical data shows behavioral and cognitive measures generally unchanged from baseline in both adults and pediatric patients — a critical differentiator from levetiracetam
  3. No titration needed in adults: Can start at a therapeutic 50mg BID dose immediately, compared to the slow titration required with many ASMs
  4. Rapid onset: Efficacy demonstrated from Day 1 in clinical trials
  5. Long-term retention: 79.8% of patients remaining on treatment at 1 year; 54.4% at 5 years (Kaplan-Meier estimates from pooled open-label studies, N=2,051)

SGTC seizure freedom data (key leave-behind message):

  • 35% overall rate of achieving >=1 year SGTC seizure freedom in 5-year follow-up
  • Earlier initiation = better outcomes: 49.4% for 0-1 prior ASMs vs 26.8% for 2-4 prior ASMs
  • Short-term (12-week): Up to 36% of patients achieved seizure freedom (BRIVIACT 200mg/day)

2.3 Competitive Positioning vs XCOPRI (Cenobamate)

XCOPRI (cenobamate, SK Life Science) is Briviact's primary branded competitor in the POS space. XCOPRI has been growing aggressively:

  • XCOPRI 2025 revenue: ~$400-500M globally (46.6% YoY growth in Q1 2025); over 100,000 patients treated globally
  • XCOPRI mechanism: Dual mechanism — sodium channel inhibition + positive GABA-A modulation
  • XCOPRI's message: "Seizure freedom" — "As many as 1 in 5 patients experienced zero seizures"; "50% seizure reduction should not be the goal"
  • Key XCOPRI advantage: The "zero seizure" message is more aggressive and aspirational than Briviact's messaging
  • Key XCOPRI disadvantage: Mandatory slow titration (weeks), REMS requirement for DRESS syndrome risk, higher AE burden

From ZoomRx PET data (Q4 2024 - Q1 2025):

  • XCOPRI leads sales rep reach (85% vs 63%) and share of voice (33-39% vs 21-26%)
  • BRIVIACT and XCOPRI message effectiveness scores are at parity but both trail neurology benchmarks (IA ~59%)
  • BRIVIACT reps are more impactful in helping HCPs identify eligible patients
  • XCOPRI reps significantly outperform BRIVIACT reps on ability to address questions, engagement, and competitor knowledge (Q1 2025 data)
  • BRIVIACT's visit frequency dropped Q4→Q1 (1.9 → 1.2), while reach held steady (~63-64%)

2.4 Key Messages — Recall and Effectiveness (from ZoomRx PET)

Most recalled BRIVIACT messages (Q1 2025):

  1. B6 — Safety: "Most AEs with BRIVIACT were reported to be mild to moderate"
  2. B7 — Retention: "Long-term retention in adult patients"
  3. B4 — Efficacy: "More than 1 in 3 patients achieved zero SGTC seizures"

Most effective BRIVIACT messages (Q1 2025):

  1. B7 — Long-term retention (ME: 50%)
  2. B3 — SGTC seizure freedom when started earlier (ME: 50%)
  3. B6 — Mild to moderate AEs (ME: 49%)

Critical issue (Q1 2025): Motivation and believability perceptions dropped across BRIVIACT messages in Q1 2025. The B2 "#1 prescribed branded ASM" message — previously the most recalled — declined to rank 4. This is a messaging fatigue signal.

2.5 Generic Threat

  • Generic brivaracetam oral solution: Lupin received USFDA approval in February 2026
  • Impact: Tablets remain patent-protected for now, but generic oral solution will pressure the pediatric segment and price-sensitive segments
  • Estimated generic pricing: $200-600/month when tablets become widely available, vs current branded pricing
  • Briviact has been growing at ~14% but this growth rate will likely moderate as generics enter

3. Nayzilam (Midazolam Nasal Spray) — Rescue Therapy

3.1 Product Profile

  • Generic name: Midazolam nasal spray
  • Mechanism: Benzodiazepine (GABA-A receptor positive allosteric modulator)
  • FDA approval: May 2019 — first and only nasal rescue treatment for seizure clusters at the time of launch
  • Indication: Acute treatment of intermittent, stereotypic episodes of frequent seizure activity (seizure clusters) in patients 12 years and older
  • Key differentiator: Single-dose nasal spray — 1 device = 1 dose. No weight-based dosing. No titration. Portable.
  • Pharmacokinetics: Peak concentration (Tmax) ~17 minutes; half-life 2-6 hours

3.2 Competitive Landscape — Rescue Therapy Market

The seizure cluster rescue market has three main branded options:

Product Active Ingredient Route Age Range 2025 Revenue
NAYZILAM (UCB) Midazolam Nasal spray >=12 years Not disclosed
VALTOCO (Neurelis) Diazepam Nasal spray >=2 years ~$295-300M
Diastat (Bausch) Diazepam Rectal gel >=2 years Declining (stigma)

VALTOCO is the primary competitor and currently dominates the rescue therapy space:

  • Neurelis reported ~$295-300M in 2025 net sales, more than doubling 2024
  • VALTOCO's pediatric expansion (ages 2-5, approved April 2025) drove 12,000+ new prescriptions
  • VALTOCO leads sales rep reach (87% vs 72%) and share of voice in the acute seizure clusters space
  • Patent protection extends through October 2032

3.3 Nayzilam's Positioning and Messaging (from ZoomRx PET)

Most recalled messages (Q1 2025, Wave 2):

  1. N8 — "Simple Dosing: 1 device = 1 dose" (63% recall, up from 51%)
  2. N5 — "80% of eligible commercial patients pay $20 or less" (63%, up from 49%)
  3. N9 — "NAYZILAM is midazolam in a portable nasal spray for seizure emergencies" (57%, up from 41%)

Most effective messages:

  • N8 (Simple Dosing) — highest ME, driven by strong believability (63%) and differentiation (56%)
  • N3 (Plan to avoid seizure cluster consequences) — most motivating to Rx (61%)
  • N7 (Stops seizure clusters fast in 10 min, back to activity in 90 min) — strong recall and motivation

Key competitive insight: Nayzilam messages are recalled and rated better than VALTOCO across matched themes (simple dosing, seizure cessation, $20 copay). But VALTOCO's broader age range (>=2 years) and larger sales force give it a structural advantage that messaging alone cannot overcome.

Nayzilam's advantage: Simpler dosing (1 dose = 1 device, no weight-based calculation), midazolam pharmacology (shorter half-life = faster return to baseline vs diazepam). Nayzilam reps also outperform VALTOCO reps on overall sales call quality.

4. Portfolio-Level Commercial Dynamics

4.1 The Multi-Product Detail — Briviact + Nayzilam

A critical operational dynamic: UCB sales reps promote BOTH Briviact and Nayzilam in a single call. ZoomRx PET data shows:

  • ~60% of time in multi-product details is spent discussing Briviact
  • ~40% spent on Nayzilam
  • This creates a tension: neither brand gets full airtime, but reps reach the same neurologist/epileptologist with both messages

4.2 HCP Target Profile

UCB targets:

  • Neurologists — primary audience for Briviact
  • Epileptologists — high-value targets for all three brands (especially Fintepla)
  • Pediatric neurologists / epileptologists — critical for Fintepla (Dravet/LGS are childhood-onset) and growing importance for Briviact (pediatric label expansion)
  • NP/PAs — increasingly important prescribers, particularly in community neurology

4.3 UCB's Strategic Direction

UCB has signaled aggressive investment in epilepsy:

  1. Neurona Therapeutics acquisition ($1.15B, April 2026): Cell therapy for drug-resistant epilepsy — interneuron transplantation (NRTX-1001). This represents UCB's bet on next-generation epilepsy treatment beyond small molecules
  2. Fintepla CDKL5 expansion: Phase 3 GEMZ trial met primary endpoint (December 2025) with 47.6% reduction in seizure frequency vs 2.8% for placebo. UCB plans regulatory filing "as soon as possible" — this would be Fintepla's third indication
  3. Revenue guidance: UCB projects high single-digit to low double-digit revenue growth for 2026, with epilepsy as a key driver

5. ZoomRx Study Landscape for UCB Epilepsy

ZoomRx conducts multiple ongoing studies across UCB's epilepsy portfolio:

Study Type Brand(s) Cadence Key Metrics
PET (Promotional Effectiveness Tracking) Briviact vs XCOPRI Quarterly Message recall, effectiveness, rep performance, SOV
PET Nayzilam vs VALTOCO Quarterly Same as above, acute seizure cluster space
SFE + Message Recall Fintepla vs Epidiolex Semi-annual (Waves) DS and LGS indication-specific, KAM performance
Non-user studies Briviact Ad hoc Barriers to trial among non-prescribers

Target HCP methodology: All data collected from UCB target HCPs (Tier 1, Tier 2, Top 35 for Fintepla; decile-based for Briviact). Sample includes neurologists, epileptologists, pediatric neurologists/epileptologists, NP/PAs.

Neurology benchmarks: ZoomRx maintains a neurology industry average (IA) from ~7,000 sales rep interactions across ~70 neurology brands. This provides context for Briviact/Nayzilam performance. For Fintepla, the benchmark is rare disease IA (~8,500-9,800 interactions).

6. Competitive Threats and Market Dynamics

6.1 Focal Seizures Market Risks

  • XCOPRI's commercial momentum: 46-52% YoY growth; first national TV campaign launched May 2026; "seizure freedom" message is more aspirational than Briviact's positioning
  • Generic erosion: Brivaracetam oral solution (Lupin, Feb 2026) is the leading edge; tablet generics will follow
  • Vimpat going generic: UCB's own lacosamide (Vimpat) went generic in 2023, accelerating the shift of neurology budgets toward branded alternatives
  • ASM market commoditization: The POS space has 20+ ASMs; differentiation is increasingly difficult

6.2 Rare Epilepsy Market Risks

  • Epidiolex's market lead: Still outsells Fintepla ~2:1 due to earlier launch (2018 vs 2020/2022) and broader indication (DS + LGS + TSC)
  • Epidiolex's orphan drug exclusivity: Expired in 2025 for DS and LGS, potentially opening the door for generic CBD products
  • Fintepla's REMS requirement: Cardiac monitoring via echocardiogram is a burden (though At-Home ECHO mitigates this)
  • Comfort gap: Only ~40% of HCPs are comfortable starting a patient on Fintepla vs higher comfort with Epidiolex

6.3 Rescue Therapy Market Risks

  • VALTOCO's commercial dominance: $295-300M in 2025 sales, doubled from 2024
  • Pediatric gap: Nayzilam's age restriction (>=12 years) vs VALTOCO's (>=2 years) is a structural disadvantage
  • Patent protection: VALTOCO protected through 2032

7. Key Numbers to Know

Metric Value Source
US epilepsy prevalence ~3.4 million CDC
Drug-resistant epilepsy rate ~30-40% ILAE
Briviact 2025 global revenue EUR758M (~$890M) UCB Annual Report
Fintepla 2025 global revenue EUR427M (~$501M) UCB Annual Report
Briviact SOV (POS space, Q1 2025) ~21-26% ZoomRx PET
XCOPRI SOV (POS space, Q1 2025) ~33-39% ZoomRx PET
Briviact rep reach ~63-64% ZoomRx PET
XCOPRI rep reach ~85% ZoomRx PET
Nayzilam rep reach ~72% ZoomRx PET
VALTOCO rep reach ~87% ZoomRx PET
Fintepla/Epidiolex SOV (DS+LGS) Comparable (~50/50) ZoomRx SFE
Neurology IA message recall ~39% ZoomRx benchmark
Neurology IA message effectiveness ~59% ZoomRx benchmark
Rare disease IA message effectiveness ~62-63% ZoomRx benchmark

3. Nayzilam (Midazolam Nasal Spray) — Rescue Therapy

Vimpat going generic: UCB's own lacosamide (Vimpat) went generic in 2023, accelerating the shift of neurology budgets toward branded alternatives
ASM market commoditization: The POS space has 20+ ASMs; differentiation is increasingly difficult

6.2 Rare Epilepsy Market Risks

Epidiolex's market lead: Still outsells Fintepla ~2:1 due to earlier launch (2018 vs 2020/2022) and broader indication (DS + LGS + TSC)
Epidiolex's orphan drug exclusivity: Expired in 2025 for DS and LGS, potentially opening the door for generic CBD products
Fintepla's REMS requirement: Cardiac monitoring via echocardiogram is a burden (though At-Home ECHO mitigates this)
Comfort gap: Only ~40% of HCPs are comfortable starting a patient on Fintepla vs higher comfort with Epidiolex

6.3 Rescue Therapy Market Risks

VALTOCO's commercial dominance: $295-300M in 2025 sales, doubled from 2024
Pediatric gap: Nayzilam's age restriction (>=12 years) vs VALTOCO's (>=2 years) is a structural disadvantage
Patent protection: VALTOCO protected through 2032


7. Key Numbers to Know
